children.name: Rutilus
children.description: Roach — one of the most abundant freshwater fish in Europe, found in virtually every Swedish lake, river, and stream from coastal Skåne to the edge of the boreal zone. The roach (mört) is a schooling, omnivorous cyprinid that feeds on aquatic plants, invertebrates, and plankton. It is a critically important prey species for pike, zander, perch, cormorants, and ospreys — the 'rabbit of Swedish freshwater ecosystems'. In eutrophic, nutrient-rich lakes roach can reach enormous densities, and their grazing suppresses aquatic vegetation, contributing to algal bloom conditions. Roach form the base of virtually every Swedish freshwater food web.

children.name: Rutilus rutilus
children.description: Arguably the most numerous vertebrate in Sweden by total individuals. Distinguished by red eyes and orange-red lower fins. Hybridises readily with bream, rudd, and white bream — hybrids are common in eutrophic Swedish lakes. Primarily a prey species but popular with match anglers.

children.name: Abramis
children.description: Bream — deep-bodied, laterally compressed cyprinids of slow rivers and lakes. The common bream (braxen) is one of Sweden's most familiar lake fish, forming large shoals that graze on benthic invertebrates by tilting almost vertically to vacuum up chironomid larvae from lake bottoms. Bream are important bioturbators: their grubbing in the sediment releases phosphorus, potentially accelerating eutrophication. Large bream ('slabs' in angler terminology) over 3–4 kg are a major target of specialist freshwater anglers. Bream are deep-bodied to avoid predation — it is difficult for a pike to swallow a fish almost as tall as it is wide.

children.name: Abramis brama
children.description: Extremely common in lowland Swedish lakes (braxen). Its benthic feeding releases sediment nutrients and can accelerate algal blooms. Large individuals can live 20+ years. Hybridises extensively with roach. Can reach 9 kg in productive waters.

children.name: Leuciscus
children.description: Ide, dace, and chub — a diverse group of European cyprinids found in rivers and lakes. The ide (id) is a handsome, fast-swimming cyprinid of clear, well-oxygenated Swedish rivers, often found in the company of grayling and trout. Its golden form ('orfe') is popular in ornamental ponds. The ide is migratory in many river systems, making large upstream spawning runs in spring — a spectacle that can be watched from bridges over Swedish spring rivers. Dace inhabit faster, cooler water than roach and are sensitive to siltation and eutrophication, making them useful water quality indicators.

children.name: Leuciscus idus
children.description: Swedish 'id' — a silvery, streamlined cyprinid of rivers and lakes. Spring spawning runs attract herons and ospreys. The golden form ('guld-id' or orfe) is popular in ornamental ponds. Tolerates slightly brackish water in the Baltic coastal zone.

children.name: Leuciscus leuciscus
children.description: A slender, active fish of fast-flowing rivers. More sensitive to pollution than roach or bream. An indicator of well-oxygenated rivers. Feeds at the surface, rising to flies in a manner that makes it beloved by fly fishers.

children.name: Scardinius
children.description: Rudd — vividly coloured cyprinids with intensely red fins and a golden sheen, among the most beautiful of European freshwater fish. Rudd are surface feeders, taking aquatic and terrestrial insects from the surface film with an upturned mouth — the opposite orientation to the downward-tilted bream. They are strongly associated with clear, weedy lakes and are considered indicators of good water quality. Swedish rudd (sarv) are found in lowland lakes with abundant submerged vegetation; their decline in some areas reflects the loss of aquatic plant communities to eutrophication.

children.name: Scardinius erythrophthalmus
children.description: Swedish 'sarv' — one of the most attractive European freshwater fish. Deep-bodied with brilliant red fins. Surface-feeding; readily takes dry flies. Associated with clear, well-vegetated lakes. Hybridises with roach; pure rudd are becoming rarer in eutrophic lakes.

children.name: Cyprinus
children.description: Common carp — one of humanity's oldest and most successfully cultured food fish, domesticated in China over 2,000 years ago and brought to Europe by the Romans. Wild carp are streamlined, golden-bronze fish of large, slow rivers and lakes; selective breeding has produced the familiar rounded, partially scaled forms of pond culture. In Sweden, carp are uncommon and largely restricted to warm, shallow lakes in Skåne and Öland. Wild carp are destructive bioturbators — their bottom-grubbing for invertebrates uproots aquatic vegetation and clouds the water, transforming clear lakes into turbid, weed-free basins. They are considered invasive or undesirable in many natural Swedish lake systems.

children.name: Cyprinus carpio
children.description: Domesticated over 2,000 years ago; one of the world's most widely cultured food fish. Wild populations in Sweden limited to warm Skåne and Öland lakes. Highly invasive in Australian freshwaters where introduced populations devastate aquatic vegetation and water clarity. The largest freshwater fish commonly encountered by Swedish anglers — can exceed 20 kg.

children.name: Carassius
children.description: Crucian carps and goldfish — stocky, deep-bodied cyprinids highly tolerant of low oxygen and ice-covered lakes. Carassius species can survive winter beneath ice by switching to anaerobic metabolism and excreting ethanol rather than lactic acid. The crucian carp is native to Europe and Asia; the goldfish and gibel carp originate from East Asia but are now globally distributed through the aquarium trade and aquaculture.

children.name: Carassius carassius
children.description: Exceptionally tolerant of hypoxia — can survive frozen, oxygen-depleted ponds all winter by producing ethanol as a metabolic byproduct. In the absence of predators, crucian carp become deep-bodied and slab-shaped to deter pike; in predator-free ponds they remain slender. Native to Scandinavia and widespread across northern Europe.

children.name: Carassius auratus
children.description: The world's most popular ornamental fish, selectively bred in China for over 1,000 years into hundreds of varieties. Wild-type goldfish are olive-green, turning golden-orange only in clear, well-lit water. Feral populations established in ponds and waterways worldwide can become significant invasive pests, hybridising with native Carassius species.

children.name: Carassius gibelio
children.description: An invasive and highly successful cyprinid originating from Siberia and China, now widespread across Europe. Females can reproduce gynogenetically, using sperm from other cyprinid species to trigger egg development without fertilisation. This unusual reproductive strategy enables rapid population growth and makes it highly competitive with native species.

children.name: Tinca
children.description: Tench — a solitary, slow-moving cyprinid of weedy, warm lowland lakes and rivers. Tench have a distinctive olive-green colouration with tiny, deeply embedded scales coated in thick mucus, giving them a smooth, almost scaleless appearance. They are famously hardy, tolerating very low oxygen levels, and in medieval times were called the 'doctor fish' in the belief that their mucus could heal the wounds of other fish.

children.name: Tinca tinca
children.description: A stout, olive-green cyprinid of warm, weedy lakes and slow rivers; Swedish 'sutare'. Nocturnal and secretive, feeding on invertebrates in muddy substrates. Its thick mucus coating gives it a slimy feel; was historically called the 'doctor fish'. Tolerates very low oxygen, surviving conditions that kill most other cyprinids.

children.name: Barbus
children.description: Barbel — large, powerful rheophilic cyprinids adapted to fast, well-oxygenated rivers. Barbel have a characteristic ventral mouth with four fleshy barbels used to probe gravel riverbeds for invertebrates. They are among the strongest freshwater fish pound for pound, making them highly prized by anglers. Barbel require clean, gravelly rivers and are sensitive to pollution and habitat degradation.

children.name: Barbus barbus
children.description: A large, powerful cyprinid of clean, fast-flowing European rivers with gravel substrates. Uses four barbels around its downward-pointing mouth to locate invertebrates and crustaceans on the riverbed. One of the hardest-fighting freshwater fish for its size; hugely popular with river anglers. Sensitive to pollution and declining in degraded rivers.

children.name: Gobio
children.description: Gudgeon — small, bottom-dwelling cyprinids of clear, gravelly rivers and streams. Despite their modest size, gudgeon are ecologically important as prey fish and are sensitive indicators of river health. They have a pair of short barbels at the corners of the mouth and a mottled brown colouration that camouflages them against gravel riverbeds.

children.name: Gobio gobio
children.description: A small, gregarious bottom-dweller of clean gravel-bottomed rivers and streams. Forms tight shoals that dash for cover when alarmed. An important prey species for kingfishers, herons, and piscivorous fish. Its presence indicates good river quality. Feeds on invertebrates, detritus, and small crustaceans.

children.name: Phoxinus
children.description: Minnows — tiny but brilliantly coloured cyprinids of cold, clear streams and mountain rivers. Male minnows in breeding condition develop vivid red bellies and green-gold flanks, making them among the most colourful native European freshwater fish. Minnows are highly social, forming dense shoals and communicating alarm through chemical signals released from damaged skin.

children.name: Phoxinus phoxinus
children.description: One of the most abundant small fish in cold, clear European streams. Shoals can number thousands of individuals that move in tight synchrony. Breeding males have brilliant red bellies and green iridescent flanks. Produce alarm pheromones from skin cells when injured, triggering flight responses in shoal-mates.

children.name: Squalius
children.description: Chub — robust, large-mouthed cyprinids of rivers and streams. Chub are generalist predators that take a wide variety of prey including insects, fish, frogs, and even small mammals that fall into the water. They are curious, opportunistic fish that will investigate almost any disturbance, making them both fascinating to observe and challenging to catch by stealth.

children.name: Squalius cephalus
children.description: A large, bold cyprinid of European rivers; Swedish 'färna'. Distinguished by its large mouth and silver-bronze scales with dark edges, creating a net-like pattern. An opportunistic generalist: takes insects, crayfish, berries, and small fish. Notoriously wary — a challenge to approach without disturbing. Reaches 4 kg in large rivers.

children.name: Alburnus
children.description: Bleak — slender, surface-dwelling cyprinids forming large, glittering shoals in lakes and slow rivers. Bleak have intensely silvery scales, historically harvested to make essence d'orient — the lustrous coating used in artificial pearl manufacture. They are important forage fish, sustaining populations of pike, perch, zander, and terns.

children.name: Alburnus alburnus
children.description: A small, intensely silver surface-dwelling cyprinid forming vast shoals in European lakes and rivers. Its scales were once harvested in huge quantities for artificial pearl manufacture (essence d'orient). An important forage fish for perch, pike, and terns. Can reach enormous population densities in eutrophic Swedish lakes.

children.name: Blicca
children.description: Silver bream (white bream) — a deep-bodied cyprinid superficially similar to common bream but smaller and with a distinctive grey-silver colour and orange-based paired fins. Often mistaken for juvenile common bream, but distinguishable by its larger eye relative to head size and paired fin coloration. A common component of mixed bream-roach shoals in eutrophic European lakes.

children.name: Blicca bjoerkna
children.description: Often called silver bream to distinguish it from common bream; Swedish 'björkna'. Smaller than common bream, rarely exceeding 35 cm. Deep-bodied and laterally compressed with orange-based pectoral and ventral fins. Found in lowland lakes and slow rivers throughout Europe. Hybridises frequently with roach and common bream.

children.name: Vimba
children.description: Vimba — an anadromous or potamodromous cyprinid that makes long spawning migrations up rivers from coastal and brackish water habitats. Vimba have an unusually protruding, piglike snout that overhangs the inferior mouth, adapted for grubbing invertebrates from gravel substrates. A prized sporting and food fish in eastern Europe and around the Baltic.

children.name: Vimba vimba
children.description: A distinctive cyprinid with a pronounced overhanging snout; Swedish 'vimma'. Makes major spawning migrations up Baltic rivers in spring and early summer. Breeding males develop vivid orange-red colouration on the belly and tubercles on the head. An important commercial and recreational fish in the eastern Baltic region.

children.name: Aspius
children.description: Asp — a large, predatory cyprinid unique among European members of the family in being an active, surface-oriented piscivore. The asp hunts by stunning small fish with violent tail-slaps at the surface before turning to engulf them — a hunting strategy that creates dramatic surface explosions audible from a distance. It occupies an ecological niche similar to a pike but in open water.

children.name: Aspius aspius
children.description: The only truly piscivorous cyprinid native to Europe. Hunts bleak and other small fish at the surface, stunning them with explosive tail-slaps before consuming them. Swedish 'asp' — found in large rivers and lakes in southern and central Sweden. Can reach 12 kg; a major target of lure anglers. Listed as Least Concern by IUCN.

children.name: Danio
children.description: Danios — small, fast-swimming cyprinids of South and Southeast Asian streams and rice paddies. Danios are among the most popular aquarium fish worldwide, and the zebrafish (Danio rerio) has become one of the most important model organisms in biomedical research. Their external fertilisation, transparent embryos, and rapid development make them invaluable for studying vertebrate genetics and development.

children.name: Danio rerio
children.description: One of the most important vertebrate model organisms in biological and medical research. Its transparent embryos, rapid generation time, and fully sequenced genome have made it indispensable for studying genetics, development, and disease. Named for its distinctive horizontal blue and silver stripes. Native to the Ganges and Brahmaputra river basins of South Asia.

children.name: Labeo
children.description: Labeos — large South and Southeast Asian cyprinids characterised by fleshy, lobed lips adapted for scraping algae and organic matter from submerged rocks and substrates. Several species are among the most commercially important freshwater food fish in South Asia, with rohu being a cornerstone of freshwater aquaculture across the Indian subcontinent and Southeast Asia.

children.name: Labeo rohita
children.description: One of the most economically important freshwater fish in South Asia, forming a cornerstone of aquaculture in India, Bangladesh, Pakistan, and Myanmar. A column feeder consuming phytoplankton, zooplankton, and submerged vegetation. Can reach 45 kg and is highly prized as a food fish. Major production exceeds one million tonnes per year from aquaculture.

children.name: Ctenopharyngodon
children.description: Grass carp — large, herbivorous cyprinids native to eastern Asia that have been introduced worldwide as a biocontrol agent against aquatic vegetation. Grass carp consume enormous quantities of aquatic plants — up to three times their body weight daily — making them useful for weed control in ponds and irrigation canals, but potentially devastating to natural aquatic ecosystems if they escape into wild waterways.

children.name: Ctenopharyngodon idella
children.description: The world's most-produced freshwater fish by aquaculture weight, with global production exceeding five million tonnes annually. A voracious herbivore that can consume its own body weight in aquatic vegetation daily. Introduced widely for weed control and food production; considered invasive in many regions where it escapes into natural waterways and strips aquatic vegetation.

children.name: Hypophthalmichthys
children.description: Silver and bighead carps — filter-feeding cyprinids that consume phytoplankton and zooplankton by straining water through specialised gill rakers. Native to large rivers of eastern Asia, they are now globally distributed through aquaculture and are considered highly invasive in North American waterways, where they outcompete native filter feeders and pose a physical hazard by leaping from the water when disturbed.

children.name: Hypophthalmichthys molitrix
children.description: A filter-feeding cyprinid that strains phytoplankton and fine particulate matter through densely packed gill rakers. One of the most produced food fish in aquaculture worldwide. Notorious in North American rivers for leaping spectacularly from the water when startled by boat engines — a hazard to boaters. Considered among the world's worst invasive fish species.

children.name: Tor
children.description: Mahseer — large, powerful cyprinids of fast, rocky rivers in South and Southeast Asia. Mahseer are among the most revered freshwater sport fish in the world, capable of powerful runs in turbulent mountain rivers. Several species are threatened by overfishing, dam construction, and habitat loss. The golden mahseer is considered sacred in some parts of India and is actively protected in temple pools.

children.name: Tor tor
children.description: A large, powerful cyprinid of fast, rocky rivers in the Himalayan foothills and Indian peninsula. Can reach 54 kg and is considered one of the ultimate freshwater sport fish, capable of explosive runs in heavy current. Listed as Vulnerable by IUCN due to overfishing, dam construction, and habitat degradation. Revered in Hindu culture and protected in some temple pools.

children.name: Pimephales
children.description: Fathead minnows and bullhead minnows — small North American cyprinids that play an outsized ecological role as forage fish and as the source of the first chemically characterised alarm substance (Schreckstoff). Fathead minnows are extensively used in toxicology testing and ecotoxicological bioassays, making them one of the most experimentally studied vertebrates in environmental science.

children.name: Pimephales promelas
children.description: A small, hardy North American cyprinid foundational to aquatic toxicology — the standard test organism for freshwater ecotoxicological bioassays under US EPA guidelines. Males develop a fleshy pad on the forehead used to clean nest sites under rocks, then guard eggs aggressively. Widely used as baitfish; can establish feral populations when released.

children.name: Garra
children.description: Garra — a diverse genus of small cyprinids found across Africa, the Middle East, and South and Southeast Asia. Characterised by a sucking disc on the underside of the snout used to cling to rocks in fast-flowing water while scraping algae and biofilm. Several species are used in the spa industry for their behaviour of nibbling dead skin from human feet, earning the name 'doctor fish'.

children.name: Garra rufa
children.description: A small cyprinid native to river systems of the Middle East, particularly Turkey, Syria, Iraq, and Iran. Famous for its use in fish spas, where it nibbles dead and softened skin from bathers' feet — a practice that spread globally from Turkish hot spring resorts. In its native rivers it feeds on algae and organic matter scraped from rocks. Wild populations are Vulnerable in their native range due to overcollection.
